expanded_description: An introduction to the cardiac cycle—the sequence of electrical and mechanical events that happens with every heartbeat. This guide explains how the heart's natural pacemaker creates electrical signals, how those signals spread through the heart to coordinate beating, the different phases of each heartbeat (contraction and relaxation), what an ECG records, and how the nervous system speeds up or slows down the heart. It also covers common heart rhythm problems and how doctors treat them.

core_concept.principle: The heartbeat is controlled by the heart's own electrical system. A tiny cluster of cells called the sinoatrial (SA) node acts as the natural pacemaker, sending out electrical signals that spread through the heart. These signals cause the atria to contract first (pushing blood into the ventricles), then the ventricles to contract (pumping blood to the body). The heart then relaxes and refills before the next beat. An electrocardiogram (ECG) records these electrical signals as waves on paper.
core_concept.explanation:
Every heartbeat follows a precise sequence. Think of it like a wave at a stadium: the SA node is the person who starts the wave; the electrical signal passes through the heart, telling the chambers when to squeeze.

The heart has four chambers: two upper chambers (atria) and two lower chambers (ventricles). For the heart to pump efficiently, the atria must squeeze first, then the ventricles.

The electrical system makes this happen:
• SA node (pacemaker): Located in the right atrium. It fires about 60-100 times per minute at rest, sending out an electrical signal.
• Atria contract: The signal spreads through both atria, causing them to squeeze. This pushes blood into the ventricles.
• AV node (gatekeeper): The signal reaches the AV node, which delays it slightly. This delay gives the atria time to finish squeezing before the ventricles start.
• Ventricles contract: The signal races down special pathways (bundle of His, Purkinje fibers) through the ventricles, causing them to squeeze from the bottom up. This pumps blood out to the lungs and body.
• Relaxation: After squeezing, the heart muscle relaxes and refills with blood for the next beat.

The electrocardiogram (ECG or EKG) is a tracing of these electrical signals:
• P wave: Atria squeezing
• QRS complex: Ventricles squeezing
• T wave: Ventricles relaxing

Fun fact: The heart generates its own electricity—it doesn't need the brain to tell it to beat. That's why a heart can keep beating even after it's removed from the body (for a short time)!

image.alt: A diagram of the heart showing the SA node (top right atrium), AV node (middle), and Purkinje fibers (throughout ventricles). An ECG strip below shows P wave, QRS complex, and T wave. Simple icons show atria contracting (P wave), then ventricles contracting (QRS), then relaxing (T wave).
image.caption: The cardiac cycle: SA node starts the heartbeat. Electrical signals spread through the heart, causing atria to contract (P wave), then ventricles to contract (QRS), then relax (T wave).

sections.section_title: Introduction: The Heart's Own Electrical System
sections.introduction:
Your heart beats about 100,000 times a day—and it does it all on its own, without you thinking about it. That's because the heart has its own built-in electrical system that controls the rhythm.

The heart's electrical system has three main jobs:
1. Generate an electrical signal at the right time
2. Spread that signal through the heart in the correct order
3. Make sure the heart contracts efficiently to pump blood

The key parts of the electrical system are:
• SA node (sinoatrial node): The natural pacemaker. Located in the right atrium, it fires about 60-100 times per minute. It sets the heart rate.
• AV node (atrioventricular node): The gatekeeper. It slows down the signal just enough to let the atria finish squeezing before the ventricles start.
• His-Purkinje network: A system of special fibers that carry the signal quickly through the ventricles, making them squeeze from the bottom up (like squeezing a tube of toothpaste from the bottom).

Why does the order matter? The atria need to squeeze first to push blood into the ventricles. Then the ventricles need to squeeze from the bottom up to push blood out to the lungs and body. If the timing is off, the heart doesn't pump as efficiently.

Doctors use an electrocardiogram (ECG or EKG) to see the heart's electrical activity. It's a painless test that uses stickers on your chest to record the signals. Each part of the tracing tells doctors something about how the heart is working.

introduction_image.alt: A simple drawing of the heart with the conduction system highlighted: SA node (top right atrium), AV node (center), bundle of His (down the middle), and Purkinje fibers (spreading through both ventricles). An ECG strip below shows the timing of each part.
introduction_image.caption: The heart's electrical conduction system: SA node (pacemaker), AV node (gatekeeper), and Purkinje fibers (deliver signal to ventricles).

key_concept.definition: The SA node generates electrical signals that spread through the atria (P wave), pause briefly at the AV node, then race through the ventricles (QRS complex), causing them to contract and pump blood. The ventricles then relax (T wave) and refill.
key_concept.context:
This sequence—atrial contraction first, then ventricular contraction—ensures that blood flows in the right direction. The slight delay at the AV node (which shows up on ECG as the PR interval) is critical. Without it, the ventricles might try to contract before the atria have finished pushing blood into them, reducing the amount of blood pumped.

Real-world connection: When doctors look at an ECG, they measure the timing between these waves. A PR interval that's too long might mean the AV node is slow (first-degree heart block). A QRS that's too wide might mean the signal is taking a detour because of a bundle branch block. A missing P wave might mean the atria aren't contracting properly (atrial fibrillation).

image.alt: An ECG tracing with labels: P wave (atria contract), PR interval (AV node delay), QRS complex (ventricles contract), T wave (ventricles relax). A simple heart diagram shows what's happening at each point.
image.caption: ECG waves: P wave = atria contract; QRS = ventricles contract; T wave = ventricles relax. The PR interval is the AV node delay.

sections.key_concept: The heartbeat has two main parts: systole (contraction, pumping) and diastole (relaxation, filling). The electrical signal triggers the mechanical contraction, and valves ensure blood flows in only one direction.

classifications.type: Electrical Conduction: How the Heart Stays in Rhythm
classifications.value: Understanding how the heart's pacemaker works, how signals spread, and what happens when the system goes wrong.
characteristics: SA Node (Pacemaker): A small cluster of specialized cells in the right atrium. They spontaneously generate electrical signals (automaticity). Resting rate: 60-100 bpm. The SA node is the fastest pacemaker, so it controls the heart rate. If it fails, other cells can take over (but at slower rates).
Atria: When the SA node fires, the signal spreads through both atria. This causes atrial muscle cells to contract, squeezing blood into the ventricles. This shows up as the P wave on ECG.

AV Node: Located between the atria and ventricles. It acts as a gatekeeper, slowing the signal down. This delay (about 0.1 seconds) gives the atria time to finish contracting before the ventricles start. On ECG, this is the PR interval.

His-Purkinje System: A network of specialized fibers that carry the signal from the AV node through the ventricles. These fibers conduct electricity very quickly (about 4 times faster than normal heart muscle). This ensures both ventricles contract at almost the same time, squeezing from the bottom up. This shows up as the QRS complex on ECG.

Automaticity: Many heart cells can generate their own electrical signals, but at different speeds. SA node: 60-100 bpm (fastest, so it's boss). AV node: 40-60 bpm (backup pacemaker). Purkinje fibers: 20-40 bpm (last resort).
classifications.behavior:
The SA node is like a drummer keeping the beat. It fires regularly, setting the pace for the whole heart. If the SA node is working normally, it's the pacemaker. If it fails, the AV node can take over (but at a slower rate), and if that fails, the Purkinje fibers can take over (even slower).

Your nervous system can speed up or slow down the SA node:
• Sympathetic nervous system (fight or flight): Speeds up the heart. That's why your heart races when you're scared or exercising.
• Parasympathetic nervous system (rest and digest): Slows down the heart. That's why your heart slows when you're relaxed or sleeping.

Clinical connection: Heart block happens when the signal from the atria doesn't get through to the ventricles properly:
• First-degree heart block: Signal is slow (PR interval too long), but every beat gets through. Often harmless.
• Second-degree heart block: Some signals don't get through. May cause dizziness or fainting.
• Third-degree (complete) heart block: No signals get through. Atria and ventricles beat independently. The ventricles beat slowly (20-40 bpm), which may not be enough to keep a person conscious. Requires a pacemaker.

examples.process: Heart Block: When the Signal Gets Stuck
examples.explanation:
Heart block is a delay or blockage in the electrical signal between the atria and ventricles: (1) First-degree AV block: The signal is slow (PR interval >0.2 seconds), but every signal eventually gets through. Most people have no symptoms and need no treatment. (2) Second-degree AV block, Mobitz I (Wenckebach): The PR interval gets longer and longer until a beat is dropped. This can cause dizziness but often doesn't need a pacemaker. (3) Second-degree AV block, Mobitz II: Beats are randomly dropped without warning. This is more serious and often requires a pacemaker. (4) Third-degree (complete) heart block: No signals get through at all. The atria and ventricles beat independently. The ventricles beat very slowly (20-40 bpm). Symptoms: severe dizziness, fainting, fatigue, shortness of breath. Treatment: permanent pacemaker.

Analogy: Heart block is like a traffic jam. First-degree: traffic is slow but moving. Second-degree: some cars get through, some don't. Third-degree: the road is completely closed; cars from one direction never make it through.

image.alt: ECG strips showing: normal (PR interval normal), first-degree block (PR long), second-degree Mobitz I (PR gets longer then dropped beat), second-degree Mobitz II (dropped beats without warning), third-degree (P waves and QRS complexes marching independently).
image.caption: Heart block types: First-degree (slow conduction), second-degree (some beats dropped), third-degree (complete block, no signals get through).

classifications.type: Mechanical Phases: Squeeze, Pump, Relax, Fill
classifications.value: Understanding what happens inside the heart during each heartbeat—the pressure changes, valve movements, and blood flow.
characteristics: Atrial Systole (Atrial Kick): The atria contract, pushing the last bit of blood into the ventricles. This adds about 20-30% more blood to the ventricles. At rest, you don't really notice it, but during exercise it becomes important. ECG: P wave. Heart sound: none normally (sometimes S4 if the heart is stiff).

Ventricular Systole (Contraction): The ventricles contract. First, pressure builds up, closing the AV valves (mitral and tricuspid). This makes the first heart sound (S1, "lub"). When pressure gets high enough, the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonic) open, and blood shoots out to the lungs and body. ECG: QRS complex.

Ventricular Diastole (Relaxation): The ventricles relax. Pressure drops, and the semilunar valves close (S2, "dub"). When pressure falls below atrial pressure, the AV valves open and blood flows from the atria into the ventricles. Most of the blood (about 70-80%) flows in passively, without the atria having to squeeze. ECG: T wave.

Heart Sounds: S1 ("lub") = AV valves closing (start of systole). S2 ("dub") = semilunar valves closing (start of diastole). S3 (extra sound) can be normal in kids but in adults may mean volume overload. S4 (extra sound) means a stiff ventricle.
classifications.behavior:
Think of the cardiac cycle like a two-stage pump:

Filling (diastole): The ventricles relax and expand like a sponge soaking up water. Blood flows in from the atria. Near the end, the atria give a little extra squeeze (atrial kick) to top them off.

Pumping (systole): The ventricles contract. First, the pressure shoots up, slamming the AV valves shut (that's the "lub" sound). Then, when pressure is high enough, the semilunar valves pop open and blood rushes out to the lungs and body. When the ventricles start relaxing, pressure drops, the semilunar valves snap shut (the "dub" sound), and the cycle repeats.

Blood flows in one direction because of the valves:
• AV valves (mitral and tricuspid): Open during filling, closed during pumping
• Semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonic): Closed during filling, open during pumping

Clinical connection: Heart murmurs are extra sounds caused by blood leaking through valves that don't close properly (regurgitation) or valves that don't open fully (stenosis). Doctors listen with a stethoscope to detect these problems.

examples.process: Heart Failure: When the Pump Fails
examples.explanation:
Heart failure means the heart isn't pumping as well as it should. There are two main types: (1) Systolic heart failure (HFrEF): The ventricles are weak and can't squeeze well. Ejection fraction (how much blood is pumped out) is low (<40%). The heart gets bigger (dilates) to try to hold more blood. (2) Diastolic heart failure (HFpEF): The ventricles are stiff and don't relax well. They can't fill properly. Ejection fraction is normal (>50%), but the heart can't accept enough blood. (3) Symptoms of both: Shortness of breath (especially when lying down), fatigue, swelling in legs (edema). (4) Causes: Heart attack, high blood pressure, valve problems, infections, genetics. (5) Treatment: Medications that help the heart pump better, remove excess fluid (diuretics), and reduce the workload on the heart.

Analogy: Systolic heart failure is like a weak water gun that can't shoot far. Diastolic heart failure is like a stiff sponge that can't soak up water—even if you squeeze it hard, there wasn't much water in it to begin with.

image.alt: Normal heart: strong squeeze, good filling. Systolic failure: weak squeeze, dilated heart. Diastolic failure: stiff heart, poor filling. Icons showing shortness of breath and leg swelling.
image.caption: Heart failure: Systolic (weak pump, dilated) vs. diastolic (stiff pump, poor filling). Both cause shortness of breath and swelling.

classifications.value: Understanding how the electrical signal (action potential) actually makes the heart muscle contract—and how certain medications affect this process.
characteristics: Action Potential: The electrical signal that travels along the heart muscle cell membrane. In ventricular cells, it has a long "plateau" phase that keeps the cell contracted long enough to pump blood out.

Calcium: The key link between electricity and contraction. When the electrical signal reaches the cell, it opens calcium channels. Calcium flows in, which triggers the cell to release even more calcium from internal stores. This calcium flood makes the muscle contract.
Contraction: Calcium binds to troponin, which moves another protein (tropomyosin) out of the way, allowing actin and myosin (the muscle proteins) to slide together and shorten the cell.
Relaxation: Calcium is pumped back into storage (sarcoplasmic reticulum) and out of the cell. When calcium levels drop, troponin releases calcium, and the muscle relaxes.
classifications.behavior:
The electrical signal is like flipping a switch. The switch (action potential) opens the door for calcium to rush in. Calcium is like the key that starts the engine—it makes the muscle fibers slide together, causing contraction. When calcium is pumped back out, the engine stops and the muscle relaxes.

This process explains why:
• The heart can't have tetanus (sustained contraction like a skeletal muscle can)—it needs time to relax and refill
• Medications that affect calcium (like calcium channel blockers) can weaken the heart's contraction or slow it down
• Digoxin (a heart medication) works by increasing calcium inside heart cells, making them contract more strongly

Clinical connection: In heart failure, the heart's calcium handling gets messed up. The cells can't pump calcium back into storage quickly enough, so relaxation is impaired. This is one reason why heart failure hearts don't fill well.

examples.process: Digoxin: A Medication That Strengthens the Heartbeat
examples.explanation:
Digoxin (also called digitalis) is one of the oldest heart medications, derived from the foxglove plant: (1) How it works: Digoxin blocks the sodium-potassium pump in heart cells. This causes sodium to build up inside the cell, which in turn reduces the activity of the sodium-calcium exchanger. More calcium stays inside the cell, so when the electrical signal comes, more calcium is available for contraction. (2) Effect: Increases the force of contraction (positive inotropy). This helps heart failure patients pump more blood. It also slows the heart rate by increasing vagal tone, which helps in atrial fibrillation. (3) Risks: Narrow therapeutic window—too much can cause toxicity. Symptoms: nausea, vision changes (yellow-green halos), arrhythmias. (4) Uses: Heart failure with reduced EF (especially if also in atrial fibrillation), and rate control in atrial fibrillation.

Historical note: Digitalis was discovered in the 1700s by William Withering, who noticed that an old woman's herbal remedy (foxglove tea) helped her heart condition. It remains in use today.

image.alt: Foxglove plant (digitalis). Diagram showing digoxin blocking the sodium-potassium pump, leading to increased calcium in the cell and stronger contraction. ECG showing slower heart rate.
image.caption: Digoxin: A medication from foxglove that increases calcium in heart cells, strengthening contractions and slowing heart rate.

sections.section_title: Key Principles of How the Heart Works

principles.principle: The Heart Is One Big Electrical Unit (Syncytium)
principles.statement: Heart muscle cells are connected by special junctions called gap junctions that allow electrical signals to pass directly from cell to cell. This means the atria work as one unit, and the ventricles work as one unit—they all contract together when the signal arrives.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• The heart contracts in a coordinated way, not like a bag of worms squirming
• Damage to part of the heart (like a heart attack) can disrupt the electrical signal, causing arrhythmias
• The signal can travel around scar tissue, creating a "short circuit" (re-entry) that causes rapid heartbeats

Everyday example: Gap junctions are like a crowd doing "the wave." When one person stands up, the people next to them see it and stand up too, passing the wave along. If some people are missing (scar tissue), the wave might have to go around them.
principles.example: After a heart attack, scar tissue forms in the heart muscle. This scar tissue doesn't conduct electricity. Sometimes, an electrical signal can travel around the scar, creating a loop (re-entry). Each time the signal goes around the loop, it re-excites the heart muscle, causing a rapid heart rhythm called ventricular tachycardia. If this rhythm becomes chaotic, it's called ventricular fibrillation—the heart quivers instead of pumping, and death can occur within minutes unless the rhythm is shocked back to normal with a defibrillator.
principles.analogy: A stadium wave: When everyone is in place, the wave travels smoothly. If a section of seats is empty (scar), the wave has to go around, potentially creating two waves that collide or a wave that goes in circles.

image.alt: A diagram showing heart muscle cells connected by gap junctions. A scar in the middle forces an electrical signal to go around it, creating a circular path (re-entry) that keeps going around and around.
image.caption: Re-entry: Scar tissue can create a short circuit where electrical signals travel in a loop, causing rapid heart rhythms (tachycardia).

principles.principle: The Frank-Starling Law: More Blood In = More Blood Out
principles.statement: The heart automatically pumps out whatever blood comes into it. If more blood flows into the heart, the heart muscle stretches more and contracts more forcefully, pumping out the extra blood. This matches the output of the right and left ventricles.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• When you exercise, more blood returns to your heart from your muscles, and your heart automatically pumps harder
• If you're dehydrated (less blood volume), your heart pumps less
• In heart failure, this mechanism is broken—the heart can't pump out the extra blood, so it backs up into the lungs (shortness of breath) or body (swelling)

Everyday example: Frank-Starling is like a rubber band. A rubber band that's stretched a little (like a heart with normal filling) snaps back with moderate force. A rubber band stretched more (like a heart with extra blood returning) snaps back with more force. But overstretch it (like a failing heart) and it doesn't snap back well at all.
principles.example: When you stand up quickly, gravity pulls blood into your legs. For a moment, less blood returns to your heart, so less blood is pumped out. That's why you might feel dizzy for a second—your brain isn't getting enough blood. But the Frank-Starling mechanism, along with your nervous system, quickly adjusts, and your heart pumps harder to restore blood flow to your brain.
principles.analogy: A trampoline: The more you sink into it (stretch it), the higher it bounces you back—up to a point. Overstretch it, and it sags.

image.alt: A graph showing stroke volume (blood pumped) increasing as end-diastolic volume (blood in the heart before contraction) increases. A normal heart (blue line) and a failing heart (red line, lower). A simple diagram showing a rubber band being stretched.
image.caption: Frank-Starling law: More blood in the heart = more blood pumped out. This works well in a normal heart but fails in heart failure.

principles.principle: The Nervous System Controls Heart Rate
principles.statement: Your heart doesn't need your brain to beat—it has its own pacemaker. But your nervous system can speed it up or slow it down depending on what your body needs.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Your heart races when you're scared, excited, or exercising (sympathetic nervous system)
• Your heart slows when you're relaxing, sleeping, or meditating (parasympathetic nervous system)
• Beta-blocker medications (like metoprolol) block the sympathetic signals, slowing the heart and reducing blood pressure

Everyday example: The heart's pacemaker is like the engine's idle speed. The sympathetic nervous system is like stepping on the gas; the parasympathetic is like easing off the gas. The engine can run without your foot on the pedal, but you use the pedal to go faster when you need to.
principles.example:
Beta-blockers are medications that block the effect of adrenaline on the heart. They slow the heart rate and reduce the force of contraction. This is helpful for:
• High blood pressure: Less force = lower pressure
• Angina (chest pain): Slower heart rate means the heart needs less oxygen
• Heart failure: Even though they weaken the heart in the short term, over time they protect the heart from the harmful effects of constant adrenaline

At first, giving a heart failure patient a medication that weakens the heart seems strange. But chronic adrenaline exposure actually damages heart cells. Beta-blockers let the heart recover.
principles.analogy: A horse: The heart is like a horse that can trot on its own. The sympathetic nervous system is like giving it a kick to gallop; the parasympathetic is like pulling on the reins to slow down. Beta-blockers are like tying the reins so the horse can't gallop too fast—good for when the horse is exhausted.

image.alt: A brain with nerves going to the heart. Sympathetic (red, speeds up) and parasympathetic (blue, slows down). A beta-blocker pill blocking the sympathetic signal. Heart rate monitor showing faster (exercise) and slower (rest).
image.caption: Autonomic nervous system: Sympathetic speeds up the heart (fight or flight); parasympathetic slows it down (rest and digest). Beta-blockers block sympathetic signals.

sections.section_title: Key Components of the Cardiac Cycle

components.definition: Electrical: SA node, AV node, bundle of His, Purkinje fibers. Mechanical: atria, ventricles, valves. ECG: P wave, QRS, T wave. Measurements: heart rate (HR), stroke volume (SV), cardiac output (CO), ejection fraction (EF).
components.equation:
Cardiac Output (CO) = Heart Rate (HR) × Stroke Volume (SV)
Ejection Fraction (EF) = Stroke Volume ÷ End-Diastolic Volume × 100%
components.interpretation:
Cardiac output is how much blood the heart pumps per minute. A normal adult at rest pumps about 5 liters per minute (about all the blood in the body). During exercise, it can pump 20-25 liters per minute.

Ejection fraction is the percentage of blood in the ventricle that gets pumped out with each beat. Normal: 55-70%. Less than 40% = systolic heart failure (weak pump).

Heart rate is controlled by the SA node and nervous system. Normal resting: 60-100 bpm. Athletes may have resting rates in the 40s—that's normal for them.

image.alt: A simple equation: Cardiac Output = Heart Rate × Stroke Volume. Icons: heart with number of beats per minute, a squeeze (stroke volume), and total blood per minute. A chart showing normal ranges.
image.caption: Cardiac output = how much blood the heart pumps per minute. Heart rate × stroke volume = cardiac output.

categories.type: The Electrocardiogram (ECG): Reading the Heart's Electrical Signals
categories.description: An ECG is a recording of the heart's electrical activity from the surface of the body. It's a quick, painless test that gives doctors information about heart rate, rhythm, and whether the heart muscle is getting enough oxygen.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
ECG leads are stickers placed on the chest and limbs. They "see" the electrical signals from different angles. The tracing shows waves that correspond to different parts of the heartbeat.

Waves and what they mean:
• P wave: Atria contracting (should be smooth and rounded)
• PR interval: Time from atrial contraction to ventricular contraction (should be 0.12-0.20 seconds)
• QRS complex: Ventricles contracting (should be narrow, <0.10 seconds)
• ST segment: Early relaxation (should be flat; elevation or depression can mean heart attack or ischemia)
• T wave: Ventricles relaxing (should be upright in most leads)
• QT interval: Total time for ventricles to contract and relax (varies with heart rate)

examples.explanation:
When a coronary artery is blocked, the heart muscle downstream starts to die. This shows up on ECG in specific ways: (1) ST elevation: The ST segment goes up like a ski jump. This usually means a complete blockage of a major coronary artery. This is called STEMI (ST-elevation myocardial infarction). It requires emergency treatment to open the artery. (2) ST depression: The ST segment goes down. This often means the heart muscle isn't getting enough oxygen but may not be fully blocked yet (ischemia). (3) Location matters: Which leads show changes tells doctors which artery is blocked. For example, changes in leads V1-V4 mean the left anterior descending artery (the "widow-maker") is blocked—a very serious situation. (4) Q waves: Deep, wide Q waves that develop after a heart attack mean the muscle has died and scarred.

Why this matters: An ECG can diagnose a heart attack within minutes of arrival at the hospital, allowing doctors to open the blocked artery quickly and save heart muscle.

image.alt: Normal ECG vs. STEMI (ST elevation like a ski jump) vs. NSTEMI (ST depression). A diagram showing which leads correspond to which coronary arteries.
image.caption: ECG during heart attack: ST elevation (STEMI) means a major artery is completely blocked and needs emergency opening. ST depression means the heart isn't getting enough oxygen.

categories.type: Arrhythmias: When the Rhythm Goes Wrong
categories.description: Arrhythmias are abnormal heart rhythms. Some are harmless; others can be life-threatening. They happen when the electrical signals are too fast, too slow, or chaotic.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Arrhythmias can be caused by:
• Abnormal pacemaker activity: Cells other than the SA node start firing (like a drummer going off-beat)
• Re-entry: Signals get stuck in a loop and go around and around, causing rapid heartbeats
• Blocked signals: Signals don't get through (heart block)

Types of arrhythmias:
• Atrial fibrillation (AFib): Most common. Atria quiver instead of contracting (no P waves). Ventricles beat irregularly. Risk: blood clots can form in the atria and cause strokes. Treatment: rate control, rhythm control, blood thinners.
• Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT): Sudden episodes of very fast heart rate (150-250 bpm). Often caused by re-entry in or near the AV node. Can often be stopped with vagal maneuvers (bearing down, coughing).
• Ventricular tachycardia (VT): Fast, regular rhythm from the ventricles. Can be life-threatening because the ventricles don't fill properly. Often requires defibrillation.
• Ventricular fibrillation (VFib): Chaotic, disorganized electrical activity. The heart quivers and doesn't pump. Leads to death within minutes unless shocked back to normal with a defibrillator.

examples.process: Atrial Fibrillation: The Most Common Arrhythmia
examples.explanation:
Atrial fibrillation (AFib) affects millions of people, and the risk increases with age: (1) What happens: Instead of the SA node controlling the beat, chaotic electrical signals swirl around the atria. The atria quiver instead of squeezing. (2) ECG: No P waves; irregularly irregular QRS complexes (the rhythm is all over the place). (3) Symptoms: Some people feel palpitations, shortness of breath, fatigue, dizziness. Many people have no symptoms at all ("silent AFib"). (4) Risks: Because the atria don't squeeze, blood can pool and form clots. If a clot travels to the brain, it causes a stroke. AFib increases stroke risk 5-fold. (5) Treatment: Rate control (medications to keep the heart rate from going too fast), rhythm control (medications or procedures to restore normal rhythm), and blood thinners (to prevent strokes).

Analogy: Normal heart rhythm is like a calm ocean with smooth waves (P waves). Atrial fibrillation is like a stormy sea with chaotic waves (no P waves, irregular beats).

image.alt: ECG: Normal (P wave, regular rhythm) vs. Atrial fibrillation (no P waves, irregular rhythm). A heart showing quivering atria. A diagram of a blood clot forming in the atria and traveling to the brain (stroke).
image.caption: Atrial fibrillation: Chaotic atria (no P waves), irregular heartbeat. Major risk: blood clots can form and cause strokes. Treatment includes blood thinners to prevent clots.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Cardiac Cycle Case Analysis

worked_examples.title: Case Study: Dizziness and Slow Heart Rate
worked_examples.problem: A 70-year-old man comes to the doctor because he's been feeling dizzy and almost fainted a few times. His ECG shows a heart rate of 38 bpm. The P waves and QRS complexes are marching independently—they have no relationship to each other. (1) What is the diagnosis? (2) Why is he dizzy? (3) What treatment does he need?

step_by_step.calculation: Heart rate 38 bpm (normal is 60-100). P waves and QRS complexes are independent (no relationship) → complete heart block (third-degree AV block). The atria are beating at their own pace (probably SA node, 70 bpm), and the ventricles are beating at their own pace (probably Purkinje escape rhythm, 30-40 bpm).

With complete heart block, the atria and ventricles don't work together. The atria contract, but the ventricles don't get the signal. The ventricles contract on their own, but slowly (38 bpm). At this slow rate, cardiac output is low. Plus, there's no atrial kick (the extra blood from atrial contraction). When he stands up or exerts himself, his heart can't increase its rate to compensate, so he gets dizzy and nearly faints (syncope).

Complete heart block with symptoms (dizziness, near-fainting) requires a permanent pacemaker. A pacemaker is a small device implanted under the skin with wires (leads) that go into the heart. It senses when the heart isn't beating and sends tiny electrical pulses to make it beat. For complete heart block, a dual-chamber pacemaker (one lead in atrium, one in ventricle) can restore normal timing (AV synchrony).

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Diagnosis: Third-degree (complete) AV block. (2) Dizziness happens because the heart beats too slowly (38 bpm) and the atria and ventricles aren't coordinated, reducing cardiac output. (3) Treatment: Permanent pacemaker implantation to restore normal heart rate and AV synchrony.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding the conduction system and the importance of AV synchrony explains why complete heart block causes symptoms and why a pacemaker is needed to restore normal function.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: The Cardiac Cycle Step by Step
sections.introduction: Here's what happens during one complete heartbeat, from electrical signal to contraction to relaxation. Follow along with the ECG, heart sounds, and blood flow.

process_flow.description: The SA node fires. The electrical signal spreads through the atria (P wave), causing them to squeeze. Blood flows from atria into ventricles.
process_flow.mechanism: Atrial contraction adds the last 20-30% of blood to the ventricles. The AV valves (mitral and tricuspid) are open; the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonic) are closed.
process_flow.result: Ventricles are now full (end-diastolic volume, EDV)

image.alt: ECG: P wave. Heart diagram: atria contracting (arrows show blood flowing into ventricles). AV valves open, semilunar valves closed.
image.caption: Step 1: Atria contract (P wave). Blood flows from atria into ventricles (atrial kick). AV valves are open.

process_flow.process: Ventricles Contract, AV Valves Close (QRS, S1)
process_flow.description: The signal reaches the AV node, delays briefly, then races down the His-Purkinje system. The ventricles contract (QRS). Pressure builds, closing the AV valves (S1, "lub").
process_flow.mechanism: Ventricular pressure rises quickly, but not yet high enough to open the semilunar valves. No blood is ejected yet (isovolumetric contraction).
process_flow.result: AV valves close (S1). Ventricles are sealed chambers with rising pressure.

image.alt: ECG: QRS. Heart diagram: ventricles contracting, AV valves closed (slamming shut = S1). Semilunar valves still closed.
image.caption: Step 2: Ventricles contract (QRS). AV valves close (S1, "lub"). Pressure builds but no blood is ejected yet.

process_flow.description: Ventricular pressure exceeds pressure in the arteries. The semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonic) open. Blood shoots out to the lungs and body.
process_flow.mechanism: Most of the blood (about 70%) is ejected rapidly, then the rest more slowly. Ventricular pressure peaks, then starts to fall.
process_flow.result: Blood pumped to lungs (right ventricle) and body (left ventricle)

image.alt: ECG: QRS (still). Heart diagram: semilunar valves open, blood shooting out to body (red) and lungs (blue).
image.caption: Step 3: Blood ejected. Semilunar valves open. Blood pumps to lungs (right side) and body (left side).

process_flow.process: Ventricles Relax, Semilunar Valves Close (T Wave, S2)
process_flow.description: Ventricles repolarize (T wave) and start to relax. Pressure drops. When ventricular pressure falls below arterial pressure, the semilunar valves snap shut (S2, "dub").
process_flow.mechanism: The ventricles are now relaxing, but the AV valves are still closed because ventricular pressure is still higher than atrial pressure. No blood enters yet (isovolumetric relaxation).
process_flow.result: Semilunar valves close (S2). Ventricles contain end-systolic volume (ESV).

image.alt: ECG: T wave. Heart diagram: semilunar valves closing (S2, "dub"). AV valves still closed. Ventricles relaxing.
image.caption: Step 4: Ventricles relax (T wave). Semilunar valves close (S2, "dub"). Ventricles are relaxing but not yet filling.

process_flow.description: Ventricular pressure drops below atrial pressure. The AV valves open. Blood flows passively from atria into ventricles.
process_flow.mechanism: Most of the blood (70-80%) flows in without the atria contracting. The cycle is now ready to start again.

image.alt: ECG: after T wave, flat line. Heart diagram: AV valves open, blood flowing from atria into ventricles. Ventricles filling.
image.caption: Step 5: Ventricles fill. AV valves open. Blood flows passively into ventricles. Cycle repeats.

sections.definition: These are more advanced concepts in cardiac physiology that show how doctors treat complex heart rhythm problems and heart failure using devices and procedures.

process_types.energy_profile: A pacemaker is an implantable device that delivers electrical pulses to the heart when the natural electrical system isn't working properly.
process_types.direction: Pacemakers have two parts: a small battery-powered generator (implanted under the skin near the collarbone) and leads (thin wires) that go into the heart. The pacemaker senses the heart's natural activity. If it doesn't detect a heartbeat within a set time, it sends a tiny electrical pulse to make the heart contract.
process_types.function: To treat symptomatic bradycardia (slow heart rate) from conditions like complete heart block, sick sinus syndrome, or certain types of heart failure.
goals: Understand why some people need pacemakers
Know the difference between single-chamber, dual-chamber, and biventricular pacemakers

primary_example.explanation: A patient with complete heart block (like the case study above) needs a pacemaker: (1) Procedure: The generator is implanted under the skin near the collarbone. Leads are threaded through veins into the heart. For complete heart block, a dual-chamber pacemaker is often used—one lead in the right atrium (senses atrial activity), one in the right ventricle (pacing). (2) How it works: The pacemaker "listens" to the atria. When it senses an atrial beat, it waits a programmed delay (to mimic AV node delay) and then stimulates the ventricle. This restores AV synchrony (the atria and ventricles work together again). (3) Types: Single-chamber (ventricle only) for chronic atrial fibrillation; dual-chamber (atrium and ventricle) for AV block; biventricular (both ventricles, also called CRT) for heart failure with bundle branch block. (4) Lifestyle: Modern pacemakers are MRI-safe, and most people can return to normal activities after recovery.

image.alt: A pacemaker (small device) under the skin near collarbone with wires (leads) going into the heart. A diagram showing a dual-chamber pacemaker sensing the atrium and pacing the ventricle. ECG before (slow, no AV synchrony) and after (normal timing).
image.caption: Pacemaker: An implantable device that restores normal heart rate and timing in patients with heart block. Dual-chamber pacemakers restore AV synchrony.

image.alt: Three pacemaker types: single-chamber (one lead in ventricle), dual-chamber (leads in atrium and ventricle), biventricular (leads in right ventricle and left ventricle via coronary sinus).
image.caption: Pacemaker types: Single-chamber (one lead), dual-chamber (two leads, restores AV synchrony), biventricular (three leads, for heart failure).

process_types.type: Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy (CRT): Fixing Heart Failure with Dyssynchrony
process_types.energy_profile: In some heart failure patients, the left and right ventricles don't contract at the same time (dyssynchrony). CRT uses a special pacemaker to make them contract together, improving the heart's pumping efficiency.
process_types.direction: In left bundle branch block (LBBB), the signal to the left ventricle is delayed. The left ventricle contracts late, and the heart squeezes inefficiently. CRT: A third lead is placed on the left ventricle (through a vein on the heart's surface). The pacemaker paces both ventricles simultaneously (or with a short delay), restoring synchrony.
process_types.function: To improve symptoms, quality of life, and survival in select heart failure patients with a wide QRS (especially LBBB) and reduced ejection fraction.
goals: Understand why dyssynchrony worsens heart failure
Know which patients benefit from CRT

primary_example.process: CRT for Heart Failure with LBBB
primary_example.explanation: A patient with heart failure (EF 25%) and left bundle branch block (QRS 160 ms) gets a CRT device: (1) Before CRT: The left ventricle contracts late. The septum (wall between ventricles) contracts first, but the lateral wall contracts later. This wasted motion reduces the amount of blood pumped (EF 25%). (2) With CRT: Both ventricles contract together. The septum and lateral wall work as one. The heart pumps more blood with each beat. (3) Results: Over time, the heart may get smaller (reverse remodeling), EF may improve, and symptoms (shortness of breath, fatigue) get better. (4) Who qualifies: Patients with EF ≤35%, QRS ≥150 ms (especially LBBB), and symptoms despite optimal medications. (5) Device: CRT-P (pacemaker only) or CRT-D (includes defibrillator for those at risk of sudden cardiac death).

image.alt: Heart with LBBB: left ventricle contracts late, dyssynchrony. CRT: leads in right atrium, right ventricle, and left ventricle (via coronary sinus). Both ventricles contract together. ECG showing wide QRS before CRT and narrower after.
image.caption: Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy (CRT): In heart failure with LBBB, biventricular pacing restores synchrony, improving pumping efficiency and symptoms.

image.alt: Normal: both ventricles contract together. LBBB: left ventricle contracts late; septum bulges; inefficient pumping. CRT: both ventricles contract together again; efficient pumping.
image.caption: Left bundle branch block (LBBB): Delayed left ventricular contraction causes dyssynchrony, reducing pumping efficiency. CRT restores normal timing.

sections.relationship: The cardiac cycle is the heartbeat itself—the perfectly timed sequence of electrical and mechanical events that keeps blood flowing. The SA node starts the cycle; the conduction system spreads the signal; the atria contract first; the ventricles contract second; and the heart relaxes to refill. The ECG records the electrical events; heart sounds mark valve closures; pressure and volume changes drive blood flow. The nervous system adjusts heart rate to meet the body's needs. When the system fails—too slow, too fast, or uncoordinated—symptoms arise. Modern medicine can fix many of these problems with medications, pacemakers, and defibrillators. Understanding the cardiac cycle helps us appreciate the elegance of the heart and the sophistication of treatments that restore its rhythm.

summary:
The cardiac cycle is one complete heartbeat—from electrical signal to contraction to relaxation. The heart has its own built-in electrical system that controls the rhythm.

Electrical system: The SA node (pacemaker) generates electrical signals (60-100 bpm). These spread through the atria (P wave), causing them to contract. The signal pauses briefly at the AV node (PR interval), then races down the His-Purkinje system through the ventricles (QRS), causing them to contract. The ventricles then repolarize (T wave) and relax.

Mechanical phases:
• Atrial systole: Atria contract, giving a final "atrial kick" to fill ventricles.
• Isovolumetric contraction: Ventricles contract, AV valves close (S1, "lub").
• Ejection: Semilunar valves open, blood pumps out.
• Isovolumetric relaxation: Ventricles relax, semilunar valves close (S2, "dub").
• Filling: AV valves open, blood flows in passively.

Key principles:
• Frank-Starling: More blood in = more blood out (heart matches output to input).
• Autonomic control: Sympathetic speeds heart (fight or flight); parasympathetic slows it (rest and digest).
• Gap junctions: Heart cells are electrically connected, so they contract together.

ECG: Records electrical activity. P wave = atria contract. QRS = ventricles contract. T wave = ventricles relax. Doctors use ECG to diagnose heart attacks, arrhythmias, and conduction problems.

Arrhythmias:
• Heart block: Signals don't get through from atria to ventricles. Complete heart block requires a pacemaker.
• Atrial fibrillation: Chaotic atria (no P waves), irregular rhythm. Increases stroke risk; often needs blood thinners.
• Ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation: Dangerous rhythms from ventricles; can cause sudden cardiac death.

Treatments:
• Pacemakers: Implanted devices that stimulate the heart when it beats too slowly.
• CRT (biventricular pacing): Helps heart failure patients with dyssynchrony (ventricles beating out of sync).
• Defibrillators: Shock the heart back to normal rhythm in dangerous arrhythmias.
• Medications: Digoxin strengthens contractions; beta-blockers slow the heart; calcium channel blockers affect calcium handling.

Understanding the cardiac cycle helps us understand how the heart works, what goes wrong in heart disease, and how treatments like pacemakers and medications restore normal function.
